name: Yamata no Orochi
description: Yamata no Orochi, meaning "eight-forked serpent" or "eight-headed serpent," is a colossal serpentine dragon from Japanese mythology. This monstrous creature is described as having eight heads and eight tails, each possessing its own unique and terrifying characteristics. Its eyes are said to be as red as winter cherries, and its belly is constantly inflamed and covered in blood. The dragon's immense size allows it to span eight valleys and eight hills, causing widespread destruction and terror. It is associated with storms, floods, and general chaos, representing a powerful force of nature that must be overcome. Its breath is poisonous, capable of withering vegetation and contaminating water sources. The slaying of Yamata no Orochi by the hero Susanoo-no-Mikoto is a pivotal event in Japanese mythology, signifying the triumph of order over chaos and the establishment of civilization.
subtitle: Eight-headed dragon of Japanese myth
appearance: A gigantic serpent with eight heads and eight tails. Its eyes are red like winter cherries, and its belly is perpetually covered in blood and inflammation. Scales are dark and shimmering.
habitat: Ancient Japan, specifically the region of Izumo. Said to dwell in caves and bodies of water.
behavior: Rampant and destructive, driven by insatiable hunger and a desire for chaos. It demands virgin sacrifices.

keyMyths.title: Susanoo's Triumph
keyMyths.description: Susanoo-no-Mikoto, banished from the heavens, encounters an elderly couple weeping over the impending sacrifice of their daughter, Kushinada-hime, to Yamata no Orochi. Susanoo devises a plan, instructing them to brew eight vats of strong sake. The dragon, lured by the aroma, consumes the sake and becomes heavily intoxicated. Susanoo then slays the dragon, severing each head and tail.

keyMyths.title: The Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi
keyMyths.description: While slaying Yamata no Orochi, Susanoo discovers the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi (Grass-Cutting Sword) within one of the dragon's tails. This legendary sword becomes one of the Three Sacred Treasures of Japan, symbolizing the emperor's power and legitimacy. The sword's discovery further emphasizes the significance of Susanoo's victory and its lasting impact on Japanese history and mythology.

keyMyths.title: Symbolism of the Dragon
keyMyths.description: Yamata no Orochi is often interpreted as a representation of natural disasters, such as floods and famines, that plagued ancient Japan. Its defeat by Susanoo symbolizes the efforts of early rulers to control these forces and establish a stable society. The eight heads and tails can also be seen as representing the eight provinces of the Izumo region, which Susanoo brought under his control.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: An English translation of the Kojiki, the oldest extant chronicle of Japan. Provides the primary account of Susanoo's encounter with and slaying of Yamata no Orochi.
sources.text: An English translation of the Kojiki, the oldest extant chronicle of Japan. Provides the primary account of Susanoo's encounter with and slaying of Yamata no Orochi.

sources.author: W.G. Aston
sources.work: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.description: An English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another ancient chronicle of Japan. Offers an alternative, though similar, account of the Yamata no Orochi myth.
sources.text: An English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another ancient chronicle of Japan. Offers an alternative, though similar, account of the Yamata no Orochi myth.

sources.author: Carmen Blacker
sources.work: The Catalpa Bow: A Study of Shamanistic Practices in Japan
sources.description: Provides an anthropological perspective on Japanese mythology, including interpretations of the Yamata no Orochi myth within the context of shamanistic beliefs and practices.
sources.text: Provides an anthropological perspective on Japanese mythology, including interpretations of the Yamata no Orochi myth within the context of shamanistic beliefs and practices.

sources.author: Miyata, Noboru
sources.work: The Origin of the Orochi Myth
sources.description: An analysis of the possible origins of the myth of Yamata no Orochi and it's link to iron production.
sources.text: An analysis of the possible origins of the myth of Yamata no Orochi and it's link to iron production.

associations.name: Eight
associations.significance: Represents abundance, multiple threats, and the complexity of chaos.

associations.name: Red
associations.significance: Symbolizes danger, blood, and the destructive nature of the dragon.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Associated with rivers and floods, representing the dragon's power to cause natural disasters.

associations.name: Mountains
associations.significance: Indicates the dragon's immense size and its connection to the earth.

associations.name: Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi
associations.significance: Represents imperial power, valor, and the triumph over evil.

associations.name: Sake
associations.significance: Symbolizes trickery, cunning, and the use of intelligence to overcome a powerful foe.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A multi-headed serpentine monster that is difficult to defeat.
A giant sea serpent representing chaos and destruction.
A multi-headed dragon that breathes fire and terrorizes the land.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings to appease water spirits
worshipPractices.description: In some regions, offerings of food and sake were made to local water deities to prevent floods and disasters, indirectly related to appeasing the dragon's destructive potential.

cultural.modernLegacy: Yamata no Orochi remains a popular figure in Japanese popular culture, appearing in video games, anime, and manga as a symbol of immense power and destruction, often serving as a formidable antagonist or a creature of legend.

extendedContent.title: Origins
extendedContent.content: The legend of Yamata no Orochi is primarily found in the Kojiki and the Nihon Shoki, the earliest written records of Japanese mythology and history. According to these texts, the dragon emerged as a terrifying force of nature, responsible for widespread devastation and fear. Its origins are somewhat vague, but it is generally depicted as a chaotic entity born from the depths of the earth or the waters, embodying primal destruction. The tale is set in Izumo Province (modern-day Shimane Prefecture), and the dragon's emergence is often linked to the local geography and natural disasters that plagued the region. The story serves as an explanation for the need for heroic intervention and the establishment of order over chaos. The dragon's insatiable appetite led it to demand a yearly sacrifice of a young maiden, further emphasizing its malevolent nature and the desperation of the local populace.

extendedContent.title: Physical Description & Powers
extendedContent.content: Yamata no Orochi is described as a colossal serpent with eight heads and eight tails, giving it a terrifying and imposing presence. Each head is said to possess unique abilities and characteristics, contributing to the dragon's overall power. Its eyes are described as being as red as winter cherries, and its belly is perpetually inflamed and covered in blood, indicating its violent and destructive nature. The dragon is large enough to cover eight hills and eight valleys, demonstrating its immense size and the scale of its threat. Beyond its physical attributes, Yamata no Orochi possesses immense strength and the ability to generate storms and floods. Its breath is said to be poisonous, and its presence brings about pestilence and famine. The combination of its physical might and supernatural powers makes it a formidable opponent, requiring exceptional cunning and bravery to defeat.

extendedContent.title: Role in Mythology
extendedContent.content: The primary role of Yamata no Orochi in Japanese mythology is as an antagonist defeated by the hero Susanoo-no-Mikoto, the Shinto god of storms and seas. Susanoo, exiled from the heavenly realm, encounters an elderly couple grieving over the impending sacrifice of their eighth daughter to the dragon. Susanoo offers to slay the dragon in exchange for their daughter's hand in marriage. He devises a plan to intoxicate the dragon by filling eight large vats with strong sake. The unsuspecting Yamata no Orochi consumes the sake, becoming heavily intoxicated and vulnerable. While the dragon is incapacitated, Susanoo severs each of its heads and tails with his sword, Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi. In one of the tails, Susanoo discovers the sacred sword, which he later presents to the goddess Amaterasu as a gesture of reconciliation. The slaying of Yamata no Orochi is a pivotal event, symbolizing the triumph of good over evil and the establishment of order in the world.

extendedContent.title: Cultural Significance
extendedContent.content: Yamata no Orochi holds significant cultural importance in Japan, representing the chaotic and destructive forces of nature. The story of its defeat by Susanoo is a powerful symbol of heroism, cunning, and the ability of humanity to overcome seemingly insurmountable challenges. The legend is deeply embedded in Japanese folklore and continues to inspire artistic expression in various forms, including traditional arts, literature, and contemporary media. The Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi, the sword discovered within the dragon's tail, is one of the Three Sacred Treasures of Japan, symbolizing the emperor's power and legitimacy. The story serves as a reminder of the importance of vigilance and the need to confront and overcome the forces of chaos to maintain peace and stability. Furthermore, the tale highlights the values of courage, intelligence, and the importance of community in the face of adversity. The legacy of Yamata no Orochi endures as a cautionary tale and a symbol of the enduring struggle between order and chaos.

shortDescription: A colossal, eight-headed serpent from Japanese mythology, symbolizing chaos and destruction, ultimately slain by the hero Susanoo.
